MTH6139 Time Series

Coursework 1 – CO2 Forecasting

Mark Aycicek

Spring term 2026

Introduction

In my coursework, I am going to analyse the CO2 levels using Meta Prophet forecasting, where I will use different plots to show the trends and seasonal patterns.

CO2 refers to atmospheric carbon dioxide concentration, which is an important indicator of climate change and greenhouse gas levels.

The CO2 dataset contains measurements of atmospheric CO2 concentrations recorded at the Mauna Loa Observatory in Hawaii. The values are measured in parts per million (ppm) and span from 1959 to 1997 with monthly observations.

CO2 Data

data(co2)

start(co2)
## [1] 1959    1
end(co2)
## [1] 1997   12
frequency(co2)
## [1] 12
summary(co2)
##    Min. 1st Qu.  Median    Mean 3rd Qu.    Max. 
##   313.2   323.5   335.2   337.1   350.3   366.8

From the summary, we see that CO2 levels have increased substantially over time, with a range of over 50 ppm between the minimum and maximum values.

CO2 Plot and Linear Regression

time_numbered <- time(co2)
linear_model <- lm(co2 ~ time_numbered)
summary(linear_model)
## 
## Call:
## lm(formula = co2 ~ time_numbered)
## 
## Residuals:
##     Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
## -6.0399 -1.9476 -0.0017  1.9113  6.5149 
## 
## Coefficients:
##                 Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
## (Intercept)   -2.250e+03  2.127e+01  -105.8   <2e-16 ***
## time_numbered  1.308e+00  1.075e-02   121.6   <2e-16 ***
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Residual standard error: 2.618 on 466 degrees of freedom
## Multiple R-squared:  0.9695, Adjusted R-squared:  0.9694 
## F-statistic: 1.479e+04 on 1 and 466 DF,  p-value: < 2.2e-16

In the linear regression summary, we see that the estimated coefficient is 1.308 meaning CO2 concentration is estimated to increase by 1.308 ppm every year in the model. You can also see the p-value is nearly 0, showing this coefficient has a strong statistical significance.

plot(co2, main = "CO2 levels over time with line of best fit")
abline(linear_model, col = "blue")

The plot shows an overall increasing trend with seasonal fluctuations for different times during each year. The blue line is the line of best fit, which represents the upward trend, created by the linear model.

Interactive Graph

library(dygraphs)
dygraph(co2, main = "CO2 Levels") %>%
  dyRangeSelector()

This plot is the same as the last one except it is an interactive graph that comes from the dygraphs package. You can zoom into specific intervals by dragging the range selector at the bottom, which allows you to see the patterns in different time periods. If you want the exact ppm for a particular month, you can just place your mouse over the graph and a green dot will show up on the line. Then, at the top, it shows the month, year and the CO2 concentration in ppm.

Log transformation

co2_log <- log(co2)
plot(co2_log, main = "Log Transformation Plot")

As shown in the plot, the log transformed series looks essentially identical to the original, implying that the variance is fairly stable throughout and a transformation is not necessary.

Preparing Prophet Data

library(zoo)
## 
## Attaching package: 'zoo'
## The following objects are masked from 'package:base':
## 
##     as.Date, as.Date.numeric
time_date <- as.Date(as.yearmon(time(co2)))

co2_data <- data.frame(
  ds = time_date,
  y = as.numeric(co2)
)

head(co2_data)
##           ds      y
## 1 1959-01-01 315.42
## 2 1959-02-01 316.31
## 3 1959-03-01 316.50
## 4 1959-04-01 317.56
## 5 1959-05-01 318.13
## 6 1959-06-01 318.00

Here I converted the CO2 time series into two columns “ds” and “y” as it is required for the Prophet functions.

Forecasting

library(prophet)
## Loading required package: Rcpp
## Warning: package 'Rcpp' was built under R version 4.2.3
## Loading required package: rlang
co2_model <- prophet(co2_data)
## Disabling weekly seasonality. Run prophet with weekly.seasonality=TRUE to override this.
## Disabling daily seasonality. Run prophet with daily.seasonality=TRUE to override this.
future_co2 <- make_future_dataframe(co2_model, periods = 120, freq = "month")
co2_forecast <- predict(co2_model, future_co2)
plot(co2_model, co2_forecast)

This plot shows the fitted model and forecast. The black dots on the line are the observed CO2 values and the section without black dots is the forecast, which you can see follows the same increasing trend. The blur around the blue line represents the confidence interval, and the blur increases with time due to uncertainty also increasing with time. To show how uncertainty increases I have chosen a long forecast time period, 10 years (120 months).

Decomposition

co2_decomposed <- decompose(co2)
plot(co2_decomposed)

This decomposition plot breaks the CO2 time series into four components, like we have in class: \[ x_t = m_t + s_t + y_t \] The observed part x_t shows the raw data, same as the original graph. The trend section m_t shows the long term increase in CO2, rising steadily from around 315 ppm in 1959 to over 360 ppm by 1997. The seasonal part s_t shows a consistent yearly cycle, where increased photosynthesis reduces CO2 levels in the sunnier months. The random section y_t shows the leftover noise after the trend and seasonality have been removed, which remains minimal throughout the series.

References

  • Queen Mary University London, MTH6139 Time Series Lecture Notes: https://qmplus.qmul.ac.uk/mod/resource/view.php?id=3114219
  • Prophet forecaster information guide: https://facebook.github.io/prophet/docs/quick_start.html#r-api
  • Dygraphs for R: https://rstudio.github.io/dygraphs/
  • Mauna Loa CO2 Data : https://scrippsco2.ucsd.edu/data/atmospheric_co2/
